1: The Philistines take the captured ark from Ebenezer (near Aphek) to Ashdod, one of the five cities of the Philistine pentapolis. Ashdod is on the coastal plain approximately 50 km southwest of Aphek — a significant transport distance for a heavy acacia wood and gold object. Placing a captured enemy's cult object in your own temple was standard ANE victory theology: the capture of a deity's image or cult object demonstrated that your god had defeated their god. Ashdod would have been the ideal site — home of Dagon's primary sanctuary.
2: The Philistines place the ark in the temple of Dagon, setting it up beside the Dagon statue. Dagon was a major Semitic grain deity (dāgān = grain) widely worshipped across Syria-Palestine — not exclusively Philistine. The Philistines adopted Dagon from the Semitic population they conquered. Archaeological excavations at Ashdod have uncovered fragments of what may be a Dagon cult image, consistent with a significant temple complex. Placing Israel's ark beside Dagon expressed the Philistine theological claim: YHWH is now Dagon's subordinate, a trophy of Dagon's superior power.
3: The next morning the Ashdodites find Dagon fallen face down before the ark of YHWH. They set him up again — the practical response of a temple staff discovering a toppled cult image. The narrative does not explain the mechanism; the night-fall of the statue is presented as a fact requiring no elaboration. The detail that Dagon is face down (ʿal pānāyw) before the ark is the ANE gesture of complete submission — a defeated vassal prostrated before a conqueror.
4: The second morning: Dagon is again fallen face down before the ark, but now his head and both his hands have been cut off and lie on the threshold, with only his torso (dāgôn) remaining. The decapitation and severing of hands are the treatment of a defeated enemy in ANE warfare — Assyrian reliefs routinely show scribes counting severed hands of the defeated. The torso labeled dāgôn may be a wordplay on the deity's name and the word for fish (dāg), or simply the term for the body trunk. The threshold becomes a sacred disgrace-site.
5: The narrative preserves a cultic etiology: the priests of Dagon and all who enter Dagon's temple at Ashdod do not step on the threshold to this day. Threshold taboos were widespread in ANE religion — thresholds were liminal spaces associated with divine or demonic presence. This specific taboo marks where Dagon's defeat was made permanent by the severed hands and head. The verse is the narrator's note that this cultic custom was still observed in his own time.
6: YHWH's hand weighs heavily on the Ashdodites, ravaging them with tumors (ʿŏpālîm). The word ʿŏpālîm in the MT denotes swellings or tumors; later scribal tradition marked it as a qere (oral reading substitute) for the written ṭĕḥōrîm. The affliction spread across the district. Modern scholars have noted that the LXX adds details about mice, and the later golden guilt-offering (1 Samuel 6:4-5) includes both mice and tumors — suggesting the disease may have been bubonic plague, transmitted by rats/mice and manifesting in swollen lymph nodes (buboes). The geographical spread along trade routes is consistent with this.
7: The people of Ashdod recognize the causal connection: 'The ark of the God of Israel must not remain with us, for his hand is harsh against us and against Dagon our god.' The acknowledgment is both practical and theological — YHWH's hand is described as affecting their deity as well as their people. The Philistine lords (sĕrānîm) are consulted — the five Philistine lords governed their cities with a collective authority structure that was distinctive in the region. 'Lord' (sārēn) may be related to Greek tyrannos.
8: The Philistine lords confer: what should they do with the ark of Israel's God? The decision to move it to Gath is the political compromise of a committee — rather than returning it, they transfer the problem to another city. Gath (Tell es-Safi) was approximately 25 km east-northeast of Ashdod, also in the Philistine heartland. The same affliction follows the ark to Gath, killing a great number there too.
9: The same affliction strikes Gath: 'the hand of YHWH was against the city, causing a very great panic, and he struck the men of the city, both young and old, so that tumors broke out on them.' The expansion of the plague to Gath, following the ark's transfer, establishes the pattern: the ark's presence is the cause, not the specific location. YHWH's 'hand' (yaḏ YHWH) is the active metaphor — the divine fist pressing down on each Philistine city in sequence.
10: The ark is sent to Ekron (Khirbet el-Muqanna, recently identified with more certainty at Tel Miqne). The Ekronites immediately object: 'They have brought the ark of the God of Israel to us to kill us and our people.' The third city already knows what happened at Ashdod and Gath; the ark's reputation has traveled faster than the cart. The cry of Ekron was 'a deadly confusion throughout the whole city' — the entire city mobilizes in protest before the ark even settles.
11: The Philistine lords assemble at Ekron: 'Send the ark of the God of Israel back to its place, that it may not kill us and our people.' The death toll at Ekron is described as 'very great' and 'the hand of God was very heavy there.' The verb used for the dying is hammaṯ (were dying), implying an ongoing process rather than a single event — consistent with disease rather than instant divine judgment. The panic is total.
12: Those who did not die were struck with tumors, and the cry of the city went up to heaven. The phrase 'cry went up to heaven' (shawaʿaṯ hāʿîr ʿālāh haššāmayim) echoes the outcry that reaches YHWH in Genesis 4:10 (Abel's blood) and 1 Samuel 18:21 (Sodom's cry). The Philistines' distress, though they are not covenant people, still rises to YHWH. The ark narrative operates within a theology of universal divine sovereignty, not merely a tribal covenant claim.
